intent: a personal dashboard for tracking long-term health experiments

body.intent: a personal dashboard for tracking long-term health experiments

draft.title: a personal dashboard for tracking long-term health experiments
draft.summary: Drafting your prototype from the swipes…
draft.html:
a personal dashboard for tracking long-term health experiments

Building your first draft…

data.intent: a personal dashboard for tracking long-term health experiments

facade.hypothesis: Users want key health metrics displayed as large, glanceable figures rather than elaborate visualizations
facade.axisTargeted: density—whether to lead with numbers or visualizations
facade.label: Big numbers, small charts
facade.acceptImplies: User prefers prominent metric values (weight, steps, sleep hours) as the primary information layer, with supporting visuals underneath
facade.rejectImplies: User prefers charts and graphs to be the primary focal point, with numbers as secondary labels or tooltips

facade.hypothesis: Users tracking health experiments prefer gentle, approachable visual language over clinical sharpness
facade.label: Soft rounded corners
facade.acceptImplies: Dashboard should feel inviting and less medical/sterile; curves over hard edges; card-based layouts with generous border radius
facade.rejectImplies: User may prefer crisp, precision-focused design; sharp corners signal clarity and data-first aesthetic; more desktop/technical feel

facade.hypothesis: Users value snappy feedback over fluid transitions when tracking health metrics
facade.label: Instant data, no animation
facade.acceptImplies: Dashboard favors speed and directness; users want numbers to appear instantly without motion; crisp, efficient interface
facade.rejectImplies: Users prefer some form of motion; animations help data feel alive; transitions matter for comprehension or satisfaction

facade.hypothesis: Users building a health dashboard prefer gentle, approachable interface elements over sharp geometry, creating psychological safety around personal data
facade.label: Soft rounded corners
facade.acceptImplies: Design leans toward organic, human-friendly aesthetics; likely favors warm tones, breathing room, and conversational language
facade.rejectImplies: User may prefer crisp, modern, or clinical presentation; possibly more minimalist grid-based layouts with confident geometry

evidence.hypothesis: Users want key health metrics displayed as large, glanceable figures rather than elaborate visualizations
evidence.implication: User prefers prominent metric values (weight, steps, sleep hours) as the primary information layer, with supporting visuals underneath

facade.hypothesis: Users prefer friendly, encouraging language over clinical tone when tracking health data
facade.axisTargeted: voice and personality — friendly vs professional
facade.label: Warm and conversational
facade.acceptImplies: User gravitates toward supportive, human tone. Consider: motivational microcopy, warm color palette, friendly illustrations, personal pronouns (you/your).
facade.rejectImplies: User prefers clinical distance or neutral tone. Consider: objective language, minimal design, data-first layout, third-person framing.

facade.hypothesis: Users want a persistent navigation sidebar (left) paired with card-based content in the main area, reducing cognitive load when switching between experiment tracking, data entry, and summaries
facade.axisTargeted: Layout structure: sidebar vs top nav; cards vs lists
facade.label: Sidebar with cards
facade.acceptImplies: User prefers vertical nav and modular content blocks — lean toward persistent left panel, card grids for experiments, spacious breathing room
facade.rejectImplies: User may prefer tabs, horizontal navigation, or dense linear layouts — explore compact list views or dashboard that prioritizes dense information hierarchy over spatial separation

axes.label: Whether user prefers minimal clinical aesthetics vs. warm, human-centered visual design for health data
Whether user wants data-dense dashboard layout vs. progressive disclosure with one metric at a time
axes.poleA: All at once or step by step?

axes.label: Whether user responds to motivational/encouraging tone vs. neutral, factual reporting voice
Whether user wants celebratory, gamified feedback vs. calm, meditative tracking environment
Whether user prefers raw numbers and charts vs. narrative summaries and insights
Whether user wants instant feedback on actions vs. slow, contemplative animations and transitions

scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Iris

scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Prism

scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Aura

scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Facet

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user wants instant feedback on actions vs. slow, contemplative animations and transitions
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Echo

facade.hypothesis: Users tracking health experiments prefer visual progress representation (bars, gauges, trends) over raw numerical values as their primary information layer
facade.axisTargeted: Information Hierarchy: visual indicators vs numerical precision
facade.label: Progress bars, no numbers
facade.acceptImplies: Dashboard should emphasize visual patterns and momentum (bars filling, arrows trending) over exact metric values, supporting users who think in terms of direction and improvement rather than absolute numbers
facade.rejectImplies: Users need numerical precision as primary information; visual progress indicators are secondary or decorative, not the main way to understand health data
